2PxFftgAAAAJ.keywords: Information Visualization, Visual Analytics, Human-Computer Interaction, Algorithm Animation, Software Visualization, Debugging and Fault Localization, Visualization Evaluation, Natural Language Interfaces for Visualization, Multivariate Data Visualization, Text and Document Visualization
2PxFftgAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
John Stasko is **Professor Emeritus of Interactive Computing** at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, where for many years he led a research group focused on **information visualization**, **visual analytics**, and **human–computer interaction**. His work spans both systems and theory: he has built practical tools and frameworks (e.g., algorithm animation systems and interactive visual analytics prototypes) while producing influential conceptual accounts of how people reason with visual representations. Across his career he has emphasized the role of <u>interaction</u>, <u>analytic activity</u>, and <u>visual representations for sensemaking</u> in enabling effective human-data and human-program understanding.

---

## Research Areas
Stasko’s research encompasses the design, evaluation, and theorization of interactive visualizations and their role in analytic work. He has made sustained contributions to <u>algorithm animation</u> (systems such as XTango and Tango and empirical studies of animation’s educational value), to visualization for software engineering (visualizations to support *fault localization* and program interaction analysis), and to the foundations of interaction in Information Visualization—articulating why and how interactive techniques matter. Other threads in his portfolio include studies of low-level <u>analytic tasks</u> and insight formation, the design of systems for investigative analysis (e.g., *Jigsaw*), ambient and casual information displays, empirical and value-driven evaluation methods, and recent work on bridging natural language and visualization (e.g., *NL4DV*). His publications combine system-building, lab-style user studies, and theoretical framing (e.g., distributed cognition) to address how visual representations and interactive techniques support real analytic goals.

---

## Notable Works
- "Visualization of test information to assist fault localization" (2002) — a highly cited contribution demonstrating how visualization can reduce developer effort in debugging by making test and coverage information more interpretable, influencing later work on program comprehension and software visualization.  
- "Toward a deeper understanding of the role of interaction in information visualization" (2007) — an influential conceptual paper that frames *interaction* as central to visual analysis and proposes ways to think about interaction beyond low-level operations, shaping subsequent research on interactive techniques and the science of interaction.  
- "Low-level components of analytic activity in information visualization" (2005) — a paper that identifies and characterizes a set of core analytic tasks people perform with visualizations, providing a task-level vocabulary that has been widely used for design and evaluation of visualization systems.

---

## Academic Background
John Stasko has spent the bulk of his academic career at the *Georgia Institute of Technology*, holding the title of **Professor Emeritus of Interactive Computing** and contributing extensively to the Institute’s research community in information visualization and HCI. Over decades he has produced highly cited work (≈29,175 citations) that spans system development, empirical evaluation, and conceptual theory, and he has contributed community resources such as datasets and toolkits that support reproducible visualization research (e.g., vispubdata.org, NL4DV). His papers appear across the visualization, visual analytics, and HCI literature (including IEEE VIS and related venues), and his body of work has influenced education (algorithm animation), software engineering visualization, investigative analysis, and the evaluation and theoretical grounding of InfoVis. He has mentored generations of students and collaborators and remains a visible figure in the visualization community; his lab and publications are archived on his faculty homepage: https://faculty.cc.gatech.edu/~stasko.

2QsddMIAAAAJ.keywords: Materials discovery, Materials informatics, Computational Materials Science, AI for Science, Single-atom catalysts, Atomically precise metal nanoclusters, Heterogeneous catalysis, Density functional theory, Graph neural networks for materials, Methane activation
2QsddMIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Victor Fung is an Assistant Professor in **Computational Science and Engineering** at the Georgia Institute of Technology and principal investigator of the Fung Group (<https://www.fung-group.org/>). His work sits at the intersection of **materials discovery**, *materials informatics*, and **computational materials science**, with a strong emphasis on bringing modern **AI-for-science** tools to bear on chemical and catalytic problems. The group combines first-principles simulation, data-driven modeling, and experimental collaboration to design and understand functional materials; signature directions include <u>single-atom and atomically precise catalysts</u>, <u>nanocluster structure–property relationships</u>, and <u>graph-based machine learning for materials</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Fung’s research program spans theory, data, and ML-enabled discovery for heterogeneous catalysis and nanomaterials. On the theory side he develops and applies density functional methods and atomic-scale modeling to explain activity and stability of supported metal species and nanoclusters (e.g., studies on single-atom Pt/Rh supports, perovskite-supported single atoms, and atomically precise Au/Pt clusters). In catalysis he has addressed methane activation and partial oxidation, oxidative dehydrogenation (ODH) of light alkanes, strong metal–support interactions (SMSI) and hydrogen spillover dynamics. On the informatics side his group benchmarks and advances machine learning architectures—most notably graph neural networks—for materials chemistry, builds physics-informed feature pipelines such as DOS-derived descriptors for adsorption energy prediction, and explores high-throughput electronic-property prediction for porous materials (MOFs). More recently the group has moved into inverse and autonomous discovery workflows, including invertible networks and large-language-model–driven design approaches to accelerate the search for synthesizable functional materials.

---

## Notable Works
- "Golden single-atomic-site platinum electrocatalysts" (2018) — Demonstrated colloidal synthesis and atomic engineering strategies to create and characterize Pt single-atom electrocatalysts; the work provided a model system linking atomic-scale structure, alloying, and catalytic performance and has become widely cited in single-atom catalyst design.
- "Benchmarking graph neural networks for materials chemistry" (2021) — Provided a systematic evaluation of GNN architectures on materials tasks, clarifying strengths and limitations of graph-based ML for predicting crystal and molecular properties and guiding best practices for materials informatics.
- "Insights into interfaces, stability, electronic properties, and catalytic activities of atomically precise metal nanoclusters from first principles" (2018) — Combined first-principles analysis with atomically resolved cluster chemistry to map structure–property relations in ligand-protected metal nanoclusters, advancing understanding of fluxionality, electronic structure, and catalytic implications of atomically precise materials.

---

## Academic Background
Victor Fung leads an interdisciplinary research group at Georgia Tech’s Computational Science and Engineering program, where he supervises students and postdocs working across theory, data science, and experiment. His publication record (total citations ~6114) reflects deep, sustained contributions to heterogeneous catalysis, nanochemistry, and materials informatics. Fung’s work is characterized by close collaborations with experimental groups (validated syntheses and characterizations of single-atom and nanoparticle catalysts) and with machine-learning researchers (methodological advances for GNNs, invertible models, and LLM-augmented discovery). His group web portal (https://www.fung-group.org/) documents ongoing projects, software, and datasets that support reproducible computational workflows and community uptake.

2a5XgNAAAAAJ.keywords: Machine Learning, Perception, Robotics, Artificial Intelligence, Few-Shot Learning, Continual Learning, Embodied AI, Semi-Supervised Learning, Out-of-Distribution Detection, Generative Adversarial Networks

33ZWJmEAAAAJ.keywords: Research areas not specified, Spoken language understanding, Task-oriented dialogue systems, Slot filling and intent detection, Deep structured semantic models (DSSM), Semantic parsing (including zero-shot parsing), Class-incremental learning (continual learning), Speaker recognition and voice authentication, Multimodal grounding (eye gaze, vision-language), Knowledge graphs and entity disambiguation
33ZWJmEAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Larry Heck is a senior researcher and faculty-affiliated scientist at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads work at the intersection of *conversational and spoken language systems*, **dialogue** engineering, and large-scale deep learning for human–machine interaction. His research program centers on building robust, scalable systems for <u>conversational understanding</u>, <u>semantic representation</u>, and <u>spoken-language interfaces</u>, combining methods from **deep neural networks**, sequence models, and pragmatic system design. Heck’s projects span fundamental model development (e.g., representation learning and recurrent architectures) and applied system-level innovations (e.g., dialogue bootstrapping, multimodal grounding, and incremental learning), emphasizing both *scientific insight* and deployable technologies.

---

## Research Areas
Heck’s body of work covers several tightly related areas in natural language processing, speech, and human–computer interaction. He has made foundational contributions to latent semantic modeling for retrieval and query–document representation (notably the Deep Structured Semantic Model/DSSM line of work), sequence and recurrent architectures for slot-filling and intent modeling in spoken language understanding, and methods for scaling semantic parsing and domain adaptation for task-oriented dialogue systems. His publications also address multimodal and pragmatic signals for conversational agents — including eye-gaze integration for disambiguation and vision–language grounding — and problems of continual and incremental learning for deployed models. Across these topics he develops and evaluates models that target both core algorithmic problems (e.g., representation learning, catastrophic forgetting) and system-level challenges (e.g., data efficiency, domain bootstrapping, self-play dialogue generation), as exemplified by papers such as "Learning deep structured semantic models for web search using clickthrough data" (2013), "Using recurrent neural networks for slot filling in spoken language understanding" (2014), and "Building a conversational agent overnight with dialogue self-play" (2018).

## Notable Works
- "Learning deep structured semantic models for web search using clickthrough data" (2013) — introduced and popularized the **DSSM** family of techniques for learning low-dimensional query and document embeddings from click signals, substantially influencing neural approaches to semantic retrieval and ranking.
- "Using recurrent neural networks for slot filling in spoken language understanding" (2014) — demonstrated how RNN architectures can effectively model temporal dependencies for slot-filling and intent extraction in SLU, helping establish sequence models as a standard approach in dialog NLU.
- "Class-incremental learning via deep model consolidation" (2020) and related work on incremental learning — addressed catastrophic forgetting in neural networks for evolving label spaces and on-device adaptation, offering practical consolidation techniques important for long-lived deployed models.

## Academic Background
Larry Heck’s publication record stretches back to the 1990s and spans both academic and industrial research venues, reflecting a long-standing engagement with speech, speaker recognition, and conversational systems. His corpus includes influential academic papers, tool releases (e.g., the MSR Identity Toolbox), and multiple patents, indicating deep collaborations with major industrial research labs as well as academic partners. With a citation footprint exceeding **15,000 citations**, Heck is recognized for shaping areas of *spoken language understanding*, *semantic modeling*, and *conversational AI*. At Georgia Tech he continues to mentor students and collaborate across disciplines, translating core research advances into scalable systems and practical dialogue technologies. For more details on publications and projects, see his homepage: https://larryheck.github.io/.

4UZP8bMAAAAJ.keywords: Cryptography, Hardware Security, Cyber Physical System Security, Intellectual Property Protection, Reverse Engineering, Hardware Obfuscation, Side-Channel Attacks, Industrial Control Systems Security, Maritime Cybersecurity, 3D Printing Security
4UZP8bMAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Animesh Chhotaray is a **Research Scientist** at **Georgia Tech**, working within interdisciplinary research groups focused on **cybersecurity**, *hardware trust*, and **cyber–physical system security**. His work centers on the security of engineered artifacts and systems — from the protection of <u>electronic-design intellectual property (IP)</u> and circuit design-hiding to the security of deployed industrial and maritime control systems and emerging manufacturing platforms. He combines empirical measurement, protocol and standard analysis, and side‑channel techniques to produce actionable findings and defenses, with an emphasis on <u>practical evaluation</u> and *threat-driven* mitigation.

---

## Research Areas
Chhotaray’s research spans three tightly coupled areas: (1) **cryptographic practice and standards** — exemplified by his teardown of IEEE P1735 where he analyzes real-world standardization mistakes and their implications for protecting IP; (2) **hardware and design‑IP protection** — including principled approaches to <u>design-hiding</u> and methods to harden circuit designs against reverse‑engineering; and (3) **cyber‑physical system security and empirical measurement** — covering automated discovery and inference of field‑deployed PLCs using large network datasets, the brittleness of device‑authentication features under ML‑based fingerprinting, and operational threat assessments for maritime systems and 3D‑printing workflows (notably optical side‑channels that can leak design files). He also contributes to engineering education through work on *Cyber‑Informed Engineering* curricula, linking technical research to workforce development.

## Notable Works
- "Standardizing Bad Cryptographic Practice: A Teardown of the IEEE Standard for Protecting Electronic-design Intellectual Property" (2017) — a systematic analysis exposing cryptographic flaws in IEEE P1735 and highlighting how standardization choices can enable insecure deployments.  
- "Hardening Circuit-Design IP Against Reverse-Engineering Attacks" (2022) — establishes a more principled foundation for design‑hiding techniques and evaluates defenses against practical reverse‑engineering threats.  
- "A Sea of Cyber Threats: Maritime Cybersecurity from the Perspective of Mariners" (2025) — an operationally grounded study that documents maritime cyber risks from practitioner viewpoints and connects those findings to broader infrastructure and supply‑chain security concerns.

## Academic Background
Chhotaray’s publication record reflects deep training in **cryptography**, **hardware security**, and **cyber‑physical system security**, and a research trajectory that moves fluidly between theoretical critique (e.g., standards analysis) and hands‑on empirical work (e.g., network‑scale inference of ICS devices and optical side‑channel experiments). He is affiliated with **Georgia Tech** as a Research Scientist and collaborates broadly with academic and operational partners in security, maritime operations, and manufacturing. His body of work has accrued over **63 citations**, and includes contributions to both peer‑reviewed research and curriculum efforts such as the *Cyber‑Informed Engineering* guides, demonstrating engagement with research, practice, and education in secure engineering.

CeEO6SIAAAAJ.keywords: Data-Centric AI, Trustworthy AI, Uncertainty, LLM, Spatiotemporal Modeling, Temporal Graph Neural Networks, Human Mobility Modeling, Programmatic Weak Supervision, Tool-augmented LLMs, Traffic Forecasting
CeEO6SIAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Chao Zhang is an **Assistant Professor** in the **College of Computing, Georgia Tech**, where he leads a research group focused on building robust, data-efficient, and trustworthy machine learning systems. His work sits at the intersection of *spatiotemporal modeling*, *graph and sequence learning*, and *large language models*, with an emphasis on practical deployment in real-world sensing and decision-making systems. Across domains ranging from traffic forecasting and human mobility to natural language and multimodal models, Zhang advances methods that quantify and reduce model *uncertainty*, improve model reliability, and support data-centric interventions. His scholarship (totaling over **18,240 citations**) spans foundational models such as graph neural networks and recurrent/attention models, as well as more recent contributions to **<u>Data-Centric AI</u>**, **<u>Trustworthy AI</u>**, **<u>Uncertainty</u>**, and the capabilities and tooling of **<u>LLMs</u>**. For more on his group and projects see his homepage: http://chaozhang.org/.

---

## Research Areas
Zhang's research program covers several tightly connected areas: large-scale spatiotemporal modeling and human mobility analysis (e.g., traffic forecasting and next-location prediction), representation learning on graphs and heterogeneous networks, weakly- and programmatic-supervision for low-label regimes, and trustworthy/deployment-aware methods for modern language and multimodal models. His early work developed specialized architectures for mobility and trajectory data (e.g., temporal graph convolutional networks and attentional recurrent models) and scalable embedding/metric-learning techniques for trajectories and heterogeneous information networks. More recent efforts pivot toward the capabilities and reliability of large language models: he has studied LLMs as generators of training data (examining diversity and bias), created datasets and benchmarks for LLM tool use and external-tool QA, and explored methods for calibration, uncertainty estimation (e.g., SDE-Net), and retrieval-augmented generation (RankRAG). Across these topics he combines model design (GNNs, RNNs, attention, diffusion/generative models), data-centric practices (programmatic weak supervision, data augmentation and selection), and evaluation methods that emphasize generalization and robustness.

## Notable Works
- T-GCN: "T-GCN: A temporal graph convolutional network for traffic prediction" (2019) — introduced a principled fusion of graph convolution with temporal sequence modeling to deliver accurate, real-time traffic forecasting and became a widely cited foundation for spatiotemporal GNNs in intelligent transportation systems.  
- DeepMove: "DeepMove: Predicting human mobility with attentional recurrent networks" (2018) — proposed attentional recurrent architectures and multi-level periodic modeling for human mobility prediction, substantially advancing next-location forecasting and trajectory representation research.  
- SALMONN: "SALMONN: Towards generic hearing abilities for large language models" (2023) — expanded LLM capabilities into general auditory perception, proposing datasets and modeling strategies that enable LLMs to perceive and reason about speech, audio events, and music, illustrating Zhang’s move into multimodal and agent-centric LLM research.

## Academic Background
Chao Zhang is an active researcher and faculty member in computing whose publication record spans over a decade of contributions to machine learning, data mining, and natural language processing. As an **Assistant Professor at Georgia Tech**, he directs projects that bridge theory, systems, and application, collaborating widely across mobility analytics, biomedical informatics, and language technologies. His work has earned broad scholarly impact (18,240 total citations) and adoption in both academic and applied settings. Zhang’s portfolio shows sustained leadership in programmatic weak supervision, spatiotemporal deep learning, graph representation learning, and trustworthy LLM methods; he regularly serves the community through peer review and program committees and maintains an active lab website (http://chaozhang.org/) that documents his students, code, and datasets.

D9jCEIkAAAAJ.keywords: Geographic Information Science, Urban Planning, Social Networks, Info Vis, Spatial Social Networks, Origin-Destination Mobility, Trajectory Data Privacy, Points of Interest (POI) Analysis, Location-Based Social Networks, Spatial Network Analysis

E2Idm8UAAAAJ.keywords: Design, Product, Education, Engineering, Assistive Technology, Accessibility, Human-Computer Interaction, Rehabilitation Technology, Inclusive STEM Education, Exergames/VR Rehabilitation
E2Idm8UAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Mahender Mandala is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, working at the intersection of *design*, *product development*, and *engineering education*. His work spans collaborations across engineering, rehabilitation science, and computing accessibility communities, with an emphasis on <u>accessible and inclusive design</u>, <u>design pedagogy</u>, and <u>technology for rehabilitation</u>. Mandala’s scholarship combines empirical user studies, design-based educational interventions, and critical human–computer interaction scholarship to advance how technologies and curricula are designed, evaluated, and adopted by diverse populations.

---

## Research Areas
Mandala’s research covers several interlocking areas. He conducts empirical and critical work in *human–computer interaction*, including disability-centered perspectives (notably in “Articulations toward a crip HCI” (2021)), and investigates user acceptance and design requirements for rehabilitation technologies and exergames for stroke survivors (“Perceptions of stroke survivors regarding factors affecting adoption of technology and exergames for rehabilitation” (2023)). He studies engineering and product design education—exploring peer review, collaboration, and scalable feedback mechanisms in large design classes (“Impact of collaborative team peer review on the quality of feedback in engineering design projects” (2018); “Comparison of Collective Team and Individual Student Peer Feedback on Design” (2017))—and develops practical, accessible computing curricula and tools for K–12 students with disabilities (e.g., “Accessible computer science for k-12 students with hearing impairments” (2020); “Accessible block-based programming for k-12 students who are blind or low vision” (2021)). Across these areas he blends design research, assistive technology evaluation, service-learning models (veteran/teacher programs), and applied clinical outcome research.

## Notable Works
- “Articulations toward a crip HCI” (2021) — A critical contribution that reframes HCI through disability studies and <u>crip theory</u>, advancing conceptual tools for researchers designing sociotechnical systems that attend to marginalization and accessibility (47 citations).
- “Perceptions of stroke survivors regarding factors affecting adoption of technology and exergames for rehabilitation” (2023) — An applied user-centered investigation into barriers and facilitators for adoption of VR/AR and exergame technologies in motor rehabilitation, informing design and clinical implementation strategies (20 citations).
- “Impact of collaborative team peer review on the quality of feedback in engineering design projects” (2018) — An evidence-driven study proposing and evaluating web-based peer-review models to scale formative feedback in design education, with implications for pedagogy and course design in large engineering classes (20 citations).

## Academic Background
Mandala’s publication record reflects a sustained engagement with engineering design, assistive technology, and inclusive STEM education since the early 2010s (papers on active cushions and assistive-device design date to 2011–2013). Now affiliated with the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, he collaborates across rehabilitation science, computer science education, and clinical research groups. His work has intersected with practitioner- and community-oriented programs—such as service-learning initiatives for veterans and teachers and NSF-supported experiential training models referenced in his program descriptions—indicating experience in funded educational outreach and translational research. With a body of work totaling approximately **142 citations**, Mandala is recognized for bridging critical HCI perspectives, practical assistive-technology development, and evidence-based innovations in engineering design education.

HmBa_6gAAAAJ.keywords: Computer Architecture, System Infrastructure Design, Machine Learning, Domain Specfic Accelerator, FPGA Acceleration, Approximate Computing, Processing-in-Memory, Recommender Systems, Device Placement Optimization, Distributed Deep Learning Training
HmBa_6gAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Divya Mahajan is a faculty researcher at **Georgia Tech** whose work bridges **computer architecture**, *system infrastructure design*, and applied **machine learning** for hardware acceleration. She leads a research effort that explores programmable and heterogeneous substrates (notably **FPGAs**, **NPUs**, and **processing‑in‑memory**) to accelerate modern ML workloads and database analytics. Her group focuses on building practical toolchains, abstractions, and runtime systems that enable the translation of high‑level ML models into efficient hardware implementations, with particular attention to <u>domain‑specific accelerators</u>, <u>approximate computing</u>, and <u>hardware/software co‑optimization</u>.

---

## Research Areas
Mahajan's research spans the intersection of architecture and ML systems. Early work developed toolflows that convert *high‑level deep neural network descriptions to FPGA implementations* ("From high-level deep neural models to FPGAs"), establishing expertise in FPGA compilation and accelerator synthesis. She has made sustained contributions to the field of *approximate computing*—both in benchmarks and language abstractions ("AxBench", "Axilog")—that quantify and manage quality‑vs‑efficiency tradeoffs. Her more recent papers address scalable and heterogeneous acceleration for large models and recommender systems (e.g., device placement and co‑design, NeuPIMs, heterogeneous pipelines for recommendation training), and system‑level concerns such as distributed/scale‑out ML training, forecasting and characterization of GPU performance, and mitigation of stragglers in federated learning. Cross‑cutting themes include <u>hardware-aware model placement and partitioning</u>, <u>compiler and template frameworks for ML kernels</u> (e.g., Tabla), and practical integrations of accelerators into database and retrieval pipelines (In‑RDBMS acceleration, learned index work).

## Notable Works
- "From high-level deep neural models to FPGAs" (2016) — a highly cited work that demonstrated a complete flow to map deep neural network descriptions to FPGA implementations, advancing practical FPGA acceleration for DNNs and influencing subsequent compiler‑driven accelerator toolchains.
- "AxBench: A Multiplatform Benchmark Suite for Approximate Computing" (2017) — introduced a diverse, reproducible benchmark suite that enabled fair evaluation of approximate computing techniques across the system stack and helped standardize comparisons in the approximate computing community.
- "Tabla: A unified template-based framework for accelerating statistical machine learning" (2016) — proposed a template‑based approach to generate efficient FPGA accelerators for a broad class of statistical ML algorithms, highlighting the benefits of domain‑specific templates and reuse for ML acceleration.

## Academic Background
Divya Mahajan has an established publication record dating back to the mid‑2010s and a total citation count exceeding **2300**, reflecting influential contributions to architecture and ML systems. She is affiliated with **Georgia Tech**, where her research is situated at the nexus of computer architecture, systems, and machine learning infrastructure. Her body of work includes collaborations and technology disclosures involving industry partners (including patent assignments and joint projects), and spans venues in architecture, systems, and machine learning. Mahajan's research has been recognized through wide scholarly citation and adoption of artifacts (benchmarks, compiler frameworks, and system designs) and she continues to shape directions in domain‑specific accelerator design, heterogeneous co‑design, and hardware‑aware ML systems.
